title: Java Basics — Structure, Main Method, Packages

sections.heading: Structure of a Java Class
The main method — entry point
sections.content:
public class Application {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Delivery system started");
        // args contains command-line arguments
    }
}
Signature must be exactly: public static void main(String[] args) • public — JVM must access it from outside • static — called without creating an instance • void — returns nothing • String[] args — command-line arguments (can also be String... args) Running from command line: 
javac DeliveryApp.java          # Compile
java DeliveryApp                 # Run (no .class extension)
java DeliveryApp --port 8080     # args[0]="--port", args[1]="8080"
Console output: 
System.out.println("line");   // prints + newline
System.out.print("no newline");  // prints, cursor stays
System.err.println("error");  // error stream (red in IDEs)
System.out.printf("Delivery %d: %s%n", id, status);  // formatted

sections.heading: Platform Independence
sections.content:
"Write once, run anywhere" Java achieves platform independence through: 1. Compilation to bytecode — javac compiles .java to .class (bytecode), not native machine code 2. JVM executes bytecode — each OS has its own JVM implementation that translates bytecode to native instructions 3. Standard library — java.io, java.net etc. abstract OS differences 
Source (.java) → Compiler (javac) → Bytecode (.class) → JVM → Native execution
                                                          ↑
                                                   OS-specific JVM
Key Java features (interview comparison): • Object-oriented — everything is inside a class (except primitives) • Strongly typed — types checked at compile time • Garbage collected — no manual memory management • Platform independent — bytecode runs on any JVM • Multi-threaded — built-in concurrency support • Secure — no pointer arithmetic, bytecode verification

sections.heading: Packages and Imports
sections.content:
Packages organize classes into namespaces and control access. 
package com.rewe.logistics.delivery;  // This class lives here

import java.util.List;                // Import specific class
import java.util.*;                   // Import all classes in package (not sub-packages)
import static java.util.Collections.emptyList;  // Import static method
Rules: • java.lang.* is auto-imported (String, Integer, System, Math, Object) • Import does NOT import sub-packages (import java.util.* does NOT include java.util.stream.*) • Two classes with same name from different packages → must use fully-qualified name for one 
// Conflict: java.util.Date and java.sql.Date
import java.util.Date;
// Must use full name for the other:
java.sql.Date sqlDate = new java.sql.Date(millis);
Package naming convention: reverse domain — com.rewe.logistics.delivery

sections.heading: Variable Scope
Primitive Types
sections.content:
Java has 8 primitive types (stored directly on the stack):
TypeSizeRangeDefaultExample
byte8 bits-128 to 1270byte b = 100;
short16 bits-32,768 to 32,7670short s = 30000;
int32 bits-2.1B to 2.1B0int i = 42;
long64 bitshuge0Llong l = 100L;
float32 bits~7 decimal digits0.0ffloat f = 3.14f;
double64 bits~15 decimal digits0.0double d = 3.14;
char16 bitsUnicode character'\u0000'char c = 'A';
boolean1 bit*true/falsefalseboolean b = true;
Widening (automatic, safe): byte → short → int → long → float → double 
int x = 5;
long y = x;    // OK: widening (int → long), no data loss
double z = x;  // OK: widening (int → double)
Narrowing (requires explicit cast, may lose data): 
long big = 100L;
int small = (int) big;   // explicit cast required
// Risk: if big > Integer.MAX_VALUE, data is truncated!

sections.heading: Reference Types vs Primitives
sections.content:
Primitives: hold the VALUE directly. Copied by value. 
int a = 10;
int b = a;   // b gets a COPY of 10
b = 20;      // a is still 10 — independent
References: hold a POINTER to an object on the heap. Copied by reference value. 
Delivery d1 = new Delivery("Route-A");
Delivery d2 = d1;        // d2 points to the SAME object
d2.setRoute("Route-B");  // d1.getRoute() is also "Route-B"!

d2 = new Delivery("Route-C");  // d2 now points to NEW object
// d1 still points to the original (now with "Route-B")
Key difference: • Primitive: independent copies, cannot be null • Reference: shared object, CAN be null • == on primitives: compares values • == on references: compares if same object (not content!)

sections.heading: Object Lifecycle & Garbage Collection
sections.content:
1. Creation: new allocates memory on the heap 
Delivery d = new Delivery();  // object created on heap, d holds reference
2. Usage: object accessible through references 
d.dispatch();  // accessed via reference d
3. Eligible for GC: when NO references point to it 
d = null;                    // reference nulled → object eligible for GC
d = new Delivery();          // reassigned → old object eligible for GC
// Method ends → local refs go out of scope → objects eligible
4. Garbage Collection: JVM reclaims memory (you cannot force it) 
System.gc();  // SUGGESTS GC, does NOT guarantee it. Never rely on this.
Key points: • You cannot manually free memory in Java (unlike C/C++) • GC runs automatically when JVM decides • finalize() is deprecated — do NOT use • Circular references ARE collected (GC uses reachability from roots, not reference counting)

sections.heading: Wrapper Classes
sections.content:
Each primitive has an object wrapper (needed for collections, null values):
PrimitiveWrapperParse methodUseful methods
intIntegerInteger.parseInt("42")Integer.valueOf(), Integer.MAX_VALUE
doubleDoubleDouble.parseDouble("3.14")Double.isNaN(), Double.compare()
booleanBooleanBoolean.parseBoolean("true")Boolean.TRUE, Boolean.FALSE
longLongLong.parseLong("100")Long.valueOf()
charCharacter—Character.isDigit(), Character.toUpperCase()
Autoboxing/Unboxing (automatic conversion): 
Integer wrapped = 42;         // autoboxing: int → Integer
int unwrapped = wrapped;      // unboxing: Integer → int

List<Integer> numbers = new ArrayList<>();
numbers.add(5);               // autoboxing: int 5 → Integer
int first = numbers.get(0);   // unboxing: Integer → int
⚠️ Trap: Unboxing null → NullPointerException 
Integer maybeNull = null;
int value = maybeNull;  // NPE! Cannot unbox null.

sections.heading: Equality: == vs equals()
sections.content:
== compares: • Primitives: VALUES (is 5 equal to 5?) • References: are they the SAME object in memory? .equals() compares: • Object CONTENT (do they have the same data?) 
// Primitives: == compares values (correct)
int a = 5, b = 5;
a == b;  // true ✓

// Strings with ==: DANGEROUS
String s1 = new String("REWE");
String s2 = new String("REWE");
s1 == s2;      // FALSE! Different objects
s1.equals(s2); // TRUE — same content

// String pool: literals are interned
String s3 = "REWE";
String s4 = "REWE";
s3 == s4;      // TRUE — same pool object (but don't rely on this!)
s3.equals(s4); // TRUE — always use this for strings
Rule: Always use .equals() for String and Object comparison. Use == only for primitives, null checks, and enum comparison.

sections.heading: if / else / ternary
sections.content:
// Standard if/else
if (delivery.isLate()) {
    notifyManager(delivery);
} else if (delivery.isAtRisk()) {
    sendWarning(delivery);
} else {
    // on time — no action
}

// Ternary operator (for simple value selection)
String label = distance > 100 ? "Long haul" : "Local";

// Ternary is an EXPRESSION (returns a value)
// if/else is a STATEMENT (executes code)
// Don't nest ternaries — becomes unreadable
Common gotcha: 
// This does NOT compile — if requires boolean
int x = 5;
if (x) { ... }  // ✗ Java is not JavaScript!
if (x != 0) { ... }  // ✓ explicit boolean expression required

sections.heading: Switch Statement
sections.content:
switch (delivery.getStatus()) {
    case PLANNED:
        System.out.println("Waiting");
        break;  // WITHOUT break → falls through!
    case DISPATCHED:
        System.out.println("On the way");
        break;
    case DELIVERED:
        System.out.println("Done");
        break;
    default:
        System.out.println("Unknown");
}

// Java 14+ switch expression (returns a value):
String message = switch (status) {
    case PLANNED -> "Waiting";
    case DISPATCHED -> "On the way";
    case DELIVERED -> "Done";
    default -> "Unknown";
};  // note the semicolon — it's an expression
Switch supports: int, byte, short, char, String (Java 7+), enum Does NOT support: long, float, double, boolean Fall-through trap: Without break, execution continues into the next case regardless of match. The arrow syntax (->) in Java 14+ does NOT fall through.

sections.heading: Operator Precedence
sections.content:
Operators evaluated in this order (highest to lowest):
PriorityOperatorsExample
1 (highest)Postfix: x++ x--i++
2Unary: ++x --x -x !!valid
3Multiplicative: * / %a * b
4Additive: + -a + b
5Relational: < > <= >= instanceofx > 5
6Equality: == !=a == b
7Logical AND: &&a && b
8Logical OR: ||a || b
9Ternary: ? :x > 0 ? x : -x
10 (lowest)Assignment: = += -=x += 5
Rule: When in doubt, use parentheses to make intent clear: 
// Unclear:
int result = a + b * c;    // is it (a+b)*c or a+(b*c)?
// Clear:
int result = a + (b * c);  // multiplication first, then add

sections.heading: One-dimensional Arrays
sections.content:
Arrays are fixed-size, indexed containers for elements of the same type. 
// Declaration + initialization
int[] distances = new int[5];           // 5 elements, all 0
int[] distances = {12, 8, 25, 30, 15};  // inline initialization
int[] distances = new int[]{12, 8, 25}; // explicit new + values

// Access (0-indexed)
distances[0];        // first element: 12
distances[4];        // last element: 15
distances.length;    // 5 (property, not method!)
distances[5];        // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException!

// Iteration
for (int i = 0; i < distances.length; i++) {
    System.out.println(distances[i]);  // by index
}
for (int d : distances) {
    System.out.println(d);  // enhanced for (no index access)
}
Key facts: • Fixed size — cannot grow after creation • .length is a field (no parentheses), not a method • Elements have default values: 0, 0.0, false, null • Arrays are objects (live on the heap, passed by reference)

sections.heading: Multi-dimensional Arrays
sections.content:
// 2D array (array of arrays)
int[][] grid = new int[3][4];  // 3 rows, 4 columns
grid[0][0] = 1;                // first row, first column
grid[2][3] = 9;                // last row, last column

// Inline initialization
int[][] matrix = {
    {1, 2, 3},
    {4, 5, 6},
    {7, 8, 9}
};

// Jagged array (rows of different lengths!)
int[][] jagged = new int[3][];
jagged[0] = new int[]{1, 2};
jagged[1] = new int[]{3, 4, 5, 6};
jagged[2] = new int[]{7};

// Iteration
for (int row = 0; row < matrix.length; row++) {
    for (int col = 0; col < matrix[row].length; col++) {
        System.out.print(matrix[row][col] + " ");
    }
}
Interview trap: Java does NOT have true 2D arrays. int[][] is an array of arrays — each "row" is an independent array that can have different lengths.

sections.heading: Loop types compared
sections.content:
LoopWhen to useChecks condition
forKnown number of iterationsBefore each iteration
for-eachIterate all elements, no index neededImplicit
whileUnknown iterations, may never executeBefore each iteration
do-whileMust execute at least onceAfter each iteration
// Traditional for — when you need the index
for (int i = 0; i < deliveries.size(); i++) {
    Delivery d = deliveries.get(i);
}

// Enhanced for — iterate all, no index
for (Delivery d : deliveries) {
    process(d);
}

// while — unknown iterations
while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
    Delivery d = queue.poll();
    process(d);
}

// do-while — always executes at least once
do {
    response = callExternalService();
} while (response.isRetry());

sections.heading: break and continue
sections.content:
// break — EXIT the loop immediately
for (Delivery d : deliveries) {
    if (d.isUrgent()) {
        processUrgent(d);
        break;  // stop searching, exit loop
    }
}

// continue — SKIP this iteration, go to next
for (Delivery d : deliveries) {
    if (d.isCancelled()) {
        continue;  // skip cancelled, process next
    }
    process(d);  // only reached for non-cancelled
}

// Labeled break — exit outer loop from inner
outer:
for (Route r : routes) {
    for (Stop s : r.getStops()) {
        if (s.isBlocked()) {
            break outer;  // exits BOTH loops
        }
    }
}
Interview tip: "Infinite loop" pattern for retry: 
while (true) {
    try { return callService(); }
    catch (TimeoutException e) {
        if (++retries >= MAX) throw e;
    }
}

sections.heading: Methods — signature, overloading, return
sections.content:
// Method signature: accessModifier returnType name(parameters)
public Delivery findById(Long id) {
    return repository.findById(id).orElseThrow();
}

// void = returns nothing
public void dispatch(Delivery delivery) {
    delivery.dispatch();
    eventPublisher.publish(delivery);
}

// Overloading = same name, different parameters
public List<Delivery> find(DeliveryStatus status) { ... }
public List<Delivery> find(DeliveryStatus status, int limit) { ... }
public List<Delivery> find(String driverName) { ... }
// Compiler selects based on argument types at call site
Overloading rules: • Same method name, DIFFERENT parameter list (type, count, or order) • Return type alone does NOT differentiate overloads • Resolution is at COMPILE-TIME based on declared argument types

sections.heading: Pass by value — the Java truth
Constructors
sections.content:
class Delivery {
    private final String id;
    private final String route;
    private DeliveryStatus status;

    // No-arg constructor
    public Delivery() {
        this("UNKNOWN", "DEFAULT");  // calls other constructor
    }

    // Parameterized constructor
    public Delivery(String id, String route) {
        this.id = id;
        this.route = route;
        this.status = DeliveryStatus.PLANNED;
    }

    // Copy constructor
    public Delivery(Delivery other) {
        this(other.id, other.route);
    }
}
Rules: • No return type (not even void) • Same name as the class • this() calls another constructor — must be FIRST statement • super() calls parent constructor — must be FIRST statement • If you write NO constructor → Java provides default no-arg • If you write ANY constructor → default disappears

sections.heading: Access Modifiers
sections.content:
ModifierClassPackageSubclass (other pkg)World
private✓✗✗✗
(default)✓✓✗✗
protected✓✓✓✗
public✓✓✓✓
Best practice (most restrictive that works): • Fields: private (always) • Methods: public for API, private for internal, protected rarely • Classes: public if used outside package • Constructors: private for singletons/factories static keyword: 
class DeliveryUtils {
    private static int count = 0;       // shared across ALL instances
    public static final int MAX = 100;  // constant

    public static Delivery create() {   // called without instance
        count++;
        return new Delivery();
    }
}
// Usage: DeliveryUtils.create(); — no instance needed

title: Exception Handling — try, catch, throw, custom

sections.heading: Exception Hierarchy
sections.content:
Throwable
├── Error (DO NOT catch — JVM problems)
│   ├── OutOfMemoryError
│   ├── StackOverflowError
│   └── VirtualMachineError
└── Exception
    ├── RuntimeException (UNCHECKED — don't require handling)
    │   ├── NullPointerException
    │   ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    │   ├── ClassCastException
    │   ├── IllegalArgumentException
    │   ├── IllegalStateException
    │   └── ArithmeticException
    └── Checked Exceptions (MUST handle with try/catch or throws)
        ├── IOException
        ├── SQLException
        ├── FileNotFoundException
        └── ClassNotFoundException
Checked: Compiler forces you to handle (try/catch or declare throws). Represent recoverable situations. Unchecked: RuntimeException subclasses. Represent programming errors. No forced handling. Errors: Serious JVM problems. Never catch these in normal code.

sections.heading: try / catch / finally
sections.content:
try {
    Delivery d = repository.findById(id);
    d.dispatch();
} catch (NotFoundException e) {
    // Handle specific exception first (most specific)
    logger.warn("Delivery not found: " + id);
    throw new BusinessException("Cannot dispatch: not found", e);
} catch (IllegalStateException e) {
    // Handle another specific type
    logger.warn("Invalid state transition", e);
} catch (Exception e) {
    // Catch-all (most general LAST)
    logger.error("Unexpected error", e);
    throw e;  // re-throw if you can't handle
} finally {
    // ALWAYS executes (cleanup)
    // Even if catch re-throws, finally runs
    metrics.recordAttempt();
}
Rules: • Catch blocks: most specific FIRST, most general LAST • finally: always executes (except System.exit()) • Multi-catch (Java 7+): catch (IOException | SQLException e) • Try-with-resources (Java 7+): auto-closes AutoCloseable resources

sections.heading: Throwing and declaring exceptions
sections.content:
// Throwing an exception
public Delivery findById(Long id) {
    return repository.findById(id)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new DeliveryNotFoundException("ID: " + id));
}

// Declaring checked exceptions (caller must handle)
public void loadFromFile(String path) throws IOException {
    Files.readAllLines(Path.of(path));  // may throw IOException
}

// Custom exception (unchecked — preferred for business logic)
public class DeliveryNotFoundException extends RuntimeException {
    private final Long deliveryId;

    public DeliveryNotFoundException(Long id) {
        super("Delivery not found: " + id);
        this.deliveryId = id;
    }

    public Long getDeliveryId() { return deliveryId; }
}
Interview tip: "When do you use checked vs unchecked?" • Checked: caller CAN and SHOULD recover (retry, alternative path). Example: IOException. • Unchecked: programming error or unrecoverable business state. Example: NotFoundException, InvalidStateException. • Modern Java trend: prefer unchecked for business exceptions.

sections.heading: Strings — Immutable
sections.content:
Strings in Java are immutable — every "modification" creates a new String object. 
String route = "Warehouse";
route.concat(" → Store");   // returns NEW string, original unchanged!
route = route.concat(" → Store");  // must reassign

// String Pool: literals are interned (shared)
String a = "REWE";   // pool
String b = "REWE";   // same pool object
a == b;  // true (same reference — but don't rely on this!)

// new String() bypasses pool
String c = new String("REWE");
a == c;   // false (different objects!)
a.equals(c);  // true (same content — always use this!)

// Common methods:
"Hello".length();            // 5
"Hello".charAt(0);           // 'H'
"Hello".substring(1, 3);     // "el" (inclusive, exclusive)
"Hello".toLowerCase();       // "hello"
"Hello World".split(" ");    // ["Hello", "World"]
"  spaces  ".trim();         // "spaces"
"Hello".contains("ell");     // true
"Hello".startsWith("He");    // true
"Hello".replace("l", "L");   // "HeLLo"
